speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry to have interrupted you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you move your chair?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't move anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. You moved this before, didn't you?

speaker: interviewer
text: I moved it again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: You'll excuse me, I cannot go away. I have to clear my throat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is tape number five with Sol Liber, L-I-B-E-R. On Thursday, August 11, 1994. Did I say tape four, or tape five?

speaker: interviewer
text: You said five.

speaker: interviewer
text: 1994, In Beverly Hills, California. And my name is Merle Goldberg. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So we worked this salt mine for a while, until the same thing happened. We could hear the artillery already. The French guys used to lie down on the ground and listen to the artillery. The ground was shaking. They used to tease the SS. You are-- you're coming to an end. So I walked over to them. I said, don't be stupid. To shoot you is just like shooting a dog right now. They're all angry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day, the the-- the Lagerfuhrer-- the-- the top SS man from-- of the lager, of the camp, comes over. We had the dinner, the big dinner you got. And he says, who wants some more food? So in German they call this [GERMAN]. Well, who doesn't want more food? I-- I-- never ran, but you know if you're hungry, take a chance. And about 60 people-- he says who wants more food-- run to the kitchen. About 60 people run to the kitchen. I was in there, including me. We came to the kitchen, the front of the kitchen. Oh, he says, [GERMAN] you criminals, he says. Don't you get enough to eat? I saw right away I made a mistake. Oh yeah? And he tells the SS to bring us to the-- to the fence on the other side of the-- on the end of the barracks. Puts everybody against the fence. And, you know, the guys came from duty, they wanted to go in a warm barrack, not stay outside and watch else. There was one guy came back from the Russian front, he had one eye, a young fellow, about 23, 24. He was angry like a dog, like a mad dog. He put on a machine gun. I said, that's-- that's how it's going to end? After I struggled so many years, you know? Somebody taps me on the shoulder and says, come out here. Come here. Gives me a bucket of paint. Until today, there are certain things I can't understand. And gives me a brush. And says, paint a cross on everybody's back. I'm going to argue with him? He has the machine gun. I go through and paint the cross on everybody's back. When I came to the end, I took the bucket and the brush, and I walked away to the barrack. Before I closed the door in the barrack, the machine gun opened, and everybody fall in one pile, you know? That's how I walked away. You get sometimes a notion, you know? I took the bucket and I walked away. If I walk away and they don't say anything, I have nothing to lose. In the morning we had to go and bury those people. They were lying stiff in the cold. We had to carry them up a hill. And how do you dig-- dig graves? In the wintertime the ground is frozen. We carried four people. We made out of boards, we made-- we carried four people in a-- in a time till we buried those people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then came the order to evacuate. The Russians were 10 kilometers-- every time was 10 kilometers, like they measured it. And they took-- took about 20 people to load the kitchen, bread, all this stuff they had in the kitchen. To go on the march, we had to be able to feed the people. And I went-- I picked the easy job. I picked to load the bread. The first bread I put in my waistband, in my waist, And put the jacket over it. And we loaded everything and we walked out. We walked out about-- the roads were already blocked with people, they were running.

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked out and there was a-- a young man, probably in his 30s. He spoke every language. He spoke from the outset, about eight languages that I know at the outset-- Italian, French, English, German, whatever. Anyway, I was told by people that he was very disobedient in the POW camp so they put him in concentration camp. I had a hunch that he was an intelligent because he spoke too many languages. Now I know it. Before, I didn't know what intelligent is, you know? They took, they held him back, and they held back a [? barbe ?], a Jewish fellow, and you hear two shots. For what and for when, I don't know, either they were getting crazy or something. These two people-- this was the first two people that we lost in the group.

speaker: interviewee
text: We marched a whole day and a whole night, because they were afraid the Russians are coming closer. The roads were so blocked. Sacks of sugar lying on the road. Cows walking. I was tired of walking. There was a truck, so I jumped on the truck. I figure maybe I'll go ahead, because I saw the end is coming, and I'll hide out somewhere for a day or two.

speaker: interviewee
text: The truck was going slower than my group. So I had to get down again. I was afraid if they catch me, they were trigger happy. So I walked down and walked with the group. We came into a city and they put us in a corral. We had-- first of all, the kitchen fell in. We worked a couple of weeks. Every time you start cooking, somebody came and said the Russians are close. They spill the food. And I was lucky, there was one SS man, he gave me to carry-- the-- you call the rucksack? [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] When you carry on your back, the-- Oh, a backpack or a knapsnack.

speaker: interviewee
text: A backpack. A backpack. He gave me to carry his backpack, and every time we took a rest, he took a-- he had a bread, cut a slice of bread. He cut a slice. When he walked away, I cut a slice for myself. And one day, he noticed that the bread is shrinking. He says [GERMAN]? What's happening here? The bread is shrinking. I say, you want me to carry your luggage, I have to have some strength to carry. You take a slice, I take a slice. Then he took out-- he-- he separated, he gave me the gun to-- the rifle to carry also. He took out the-- what you call it, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the case that holds the bullets. He took out the case, and he let me carry this also. So on the end, we came into a city, it called Annenberg. And we're sitting in a corral. We didn't eat for a few days because every time, they spilled the food and told us to march on. And we sit there, and nothing's happening. We have no food. The kitchen fell into the Russians. The last night, the kitchen-- the Russian came so fast, that the kitchen had no chance to run away. The kitchen and the horses, that was a horse-drawn kitchen. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a German Jew hiding out in the city through the war. He heard-- he saw the situation. He heard some Jewish people are sitting here. He went to the burgermeister, to the mayor of the city. He said, why don't you give them some food? There's some concentration camp people that need some food. So he put him in jail. The day that the city was liberated, before the city was liberated.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now the SS said, we have to get some food. We cannot take these people marching again without food. So they try to go to the city and see if they could get some food. They came to the road, and they saw Russian tanks already. The day before we left the camp, they prepare civilian clothes. The clothes that we should get, they cleaned it, and they had tailors there to fit them. And when we came back, the Russians are here. They changed their-- either they wore it underneath the uniforms. It took us seconds. You didn't see anybody, they all escaped. And so we start walking down to the city. There was a park, horses and wagons, food. I took a bread, and I remember, and-- and a pack of honey. And I sat down and I ate. I said to her now, the whole world could turn around. We had holes in our feet, bleeding from walking so many weeks. As we were walking, the French people were falling like flies. See in the camp, they-- they had their own kitchen. They used to get more food than we used to get. And they also had a habit, they used to dig some kind of a lettuce or grass from the ground with a spoon. With the dirt they used to eat, and they used to get diarrhea. And I figured it's better to be hungry. I'll survive rather than picking this stuff, you know, and eat it with dirt and everything. We started out with about 3,100 people. When we were liberated, we had 1,900. 1,200 people died in a few weeks on the way. We were liberated. We went to the city. We had to-- so the city-- they didn't know who the city was assigned yet. We had-- the Americans were spearheaded, and the Russians came in. And one-- and one guy, an elderly person, you know, if there were any older than I was, he was asking about Jews. If there's a Jewish in the-- in the American group.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they were laughing, you know, because he didn't pronounce it right. And he-- the captain went to talk to the Russian. He was-- he came in as a boy from the Russian-- from Russia, and he knew Russian and English so he was the-- he was a captain. And the soldiers told him that we are asking for a Jewish person. So he came around, and we asked him if he could help us get some-- establish some kitchen for the people. He went to the Russians. They established a kitchen about two, three hours. Start cooking food that we shouldn't get sick, rice and all cereals kind of stuff. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I stayed two weeks-- two weeks in the city, and I was restless. You know, when-- you never believe that everybody's gone. So I say to another guy, you want to go back to Poland? We went back to Poland. The bridges were torn apart. We had to go on a step ladder down, a step ladder up.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to Poland. From all the 5,200 people, we had 12 people sleeping on a floor in one room. I want to make it short because we're working on the last tape. After a couple of weeks, the Polish underground, the anti-Semites came in and told us to get out or they'll kill us. Ten of us left, two stayed. They wanted-- they had to take back some buildings there in the city. They wanted to take back. They were shot. And I went back-- I went back to Germany. I was told-- somebody came this group-- they told me my brother's alive. So I went back to Germany. And I stayed in Germany. I found my brother, and I stayed in Germany three years. I couldn't take it anymore. So I registered to go to Israel in 1948 when they were fighting. You know, my brother always told me, you always want to go to hell. Always where there is fire, you want to go. I said, you do what you want to do, I do what I want to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live in Germany, with your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: We took away a couple of hotels, German hotels, and we-- we displaced the people. There wasn't a big amount of people there.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a DP camp, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah there was an UNRRA camp there, while I was in the police.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about that quickly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and I-- I came to Strasbourg. I mailed a card to my cousin that if she wants to come to see me, I'm going to Israel, and I'm going to be in- in-- Marseille. In Camp [FRENCH]. Well, she was on vacation and by luck, she came into Paris, and she found the card, and she came to see me. And she said, you're not going to Israel because you remained alone. You have to build a family. You're not going to get killed now again, you gave enough already.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she took me to Paris. Making it short, she took me to Paris, and I went to work in Paris. I had no papers. They took me to the city hall. They made me working papers and temporary papers to stay. I stayed in Paris about a year and my brother sent me papers to come to Canada. Your brother who had been in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he got-- I couldn't wait. I was registered also, and we had papers to go to the United States. I didn't want to wait anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he had already gone to Canada.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: He sent me papers and I came to Canada. And I came into Winnipeg to a city. It's like in Siberia. After seven months, I went to Montreal. In Montreal, life was nicer. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I met my wife. In 1953, we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bella. In 1953, we got married. I was still restless. I said, I don't want to stay in Montreal. I said, I want to go to California. I says, what makes you think you've never been to California? I said, at least I hear it's a warm climate and I want to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you making a living in Montreal?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was working as an operator, machine operator, a tailor, ladies clothes. And I came to-- I left Montreal, it was about 25 below zero Christmas-- Christmas morning-- Christmas Day, in the morning. We came to California, and it took me about three months to get a job. We rent an apartment. We came with-- my older son was a year and a half, and my girl was six weeks old. Everybody said I'm crazy that I'm leaving with little kids. We came here, and I never regret it. I worked here also as an operator for 12 years. I worked on the Rodeo Drive for all the actresses and--

speaker: interviewer
text: As a tailor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And then one day when my third boy was born, I asked--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was when?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in-- must have been about 1963. My third boy was born. I asked my boss to give me a quarter more. I need more. I have to feed a third mouth. He says he can't afford it. I say, you can't afford it? I finished out the work. I got up, and I said, adios. And I said to my wife, I came home, I said, I never go back working again. I have to look for some business. Whatever I do, but I don't go back to work the machine. I walked around for six months. For six months I walked around, and then I bought in a partnership in an old broken down factory. And the rest is history. I worked up the factory to about 100 people worked for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of factory was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ladies jackets, dresses. I paid out my partner because he had no idea on the tailoring business. So I paid him out, and I remained myself. And I build up, build up, from-- from 20 people to about almost 100 people. And in-- this is 14 years ago-- 1980, I sold the factory, and I semi-retired. And now, I'm just managing my own property. I have some property downtown. This is all I do right now. And whoever sees this tape, if I missed something, sorry. I bet I could sit another two hours here.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have one question for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now that it's almost 50 years since the end of the war--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is it that-- what is-- what message or lesson do you want pass on to your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- my grandchildren, I hope they will never forget, if they see the tape. And if it comes to fight back, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] die as a hero, not as a-- what would I call the opposite to a hero? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Die as a hero, and don't suffer as I suffered through those years. And I love you all. You'll be-- you were all nice. And to backtrack about my-- you asked a question about my parents and my younger sister. They were shipped to Treblinka about a year before me. That will give you an idea that-- and with this I'll end. Thank you for making the tape. I unloaded. Maybe I'll feel better. It was all inside for years. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, on the left side is my mother. On the right side is my father. And the middle is my Uncle Ishmuel that died in the first bombing of the flour mill. The baby, I don't know which one it is. I can't recognize it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Those are my two sisters and brothers. To the right, my brother Yitzak, Rafael, Tishel, and Schayva. This picture was saved for me by my grandmother in Chicago. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me. I was a year old. And this was also saved by my family in Chicago. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This picture-- this picture was given to me by my aunt in Paris. This is my Uncle Israel, my Aunt Cheryl, my cousin Tishel, and [? Madeleine. ?] Rafael, [? Moshe, ?] Yitzak, and Schlomo.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got them all. They all went to Treblinka in the same transport when I was there. They all died in the gas chamber. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my mother, Shayndel. This is my cousin, [? Shoel, ?] and this is his wife, which I don't remember the name. The picture was saved in Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right in the middle.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my identification. I belonged to the Youth of the Betar. And this was given to me in 1945, if I'm not mistaken, in Germany, the city of Eggenfelden.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my identification given by the Warsaw Committee as a fighter in the Warsaw ghetto. It was given to me in Germany, in the city of Munich. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my police identification in the UNRRA camp in Eggenfelden, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Explain what-- what this was.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- we had our own police in our camp we were stationed. And we were guarding our buildings, and we were not-- had-- we had nothing to do with the German police.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war, yes. [INTERPOSING VOICES] Yeah, stay here, and don't make faces. Don't make faces. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, great. Now, this is a great family.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, now why don't you tell us who everybody is here?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, who wants to tell from the back there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it's you, it's you.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have to look back, OK. Sheldon here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, everybody's here.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, my oldest son, Sheldon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Raise your hand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, raise the hand.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, Sheldon's two kids are here. Here is Jacob, and here is Elizabeth. Jacob and Elizabeth. Then Sheldon's wife, Chelsey.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And here is Susan, my daughter. And here is Tamara.

speaker: interviewer
text: Raise your hand.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Daniel and Becky.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's OK, it's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did we get everybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: Did we get Elizabee?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's this guy in the back?

speaker: interviewee
text: This guy in the back is last. Well this is my wife, my sweetheart, Bella. And here, my youngest Rodney. You can see I'm hoarse from talking.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what about Elizabeth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Elizabeth we did already. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you want to say while you guys are--

speaker: interviewee
text: Say to you?

speaker: interviewer
text: All together right here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you want  to say while you have them all together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, well, I cheated Hitler and his henchmen, and this is what I got right now here, a lovely family, my wife and my kids. And this is the happiest moment of my life because I unloaded everything on tape. Maybe I'm not going to be so crazy anymore. [LAUGHTER] OK, he wants to run. You better keep him till they take out this-- till they take out--
